CSCE 411 Syllabus

Section 550 (51250)

Design and Analysis of Algorithms

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 09:35AM

End Time: 10:50AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  CLB 114

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Han Tran

Email:  hantran@tamu.edu

Office Location:  ECRB 309D

Phone:  409-740-5517

Office Hours

MW 11:00 AM - 12:00 PM

Catalog Description

Design and Analysis of Algorithms. (3-0). Credit 3. Study of computer algorithms for

numeric and non-numeric problems; design paradigms; analysis of time and space

requirements of algorithms; correctness of algorithms; NP-completeness and

undecidability of problems. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 221 and

CSCE 222; junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221 and

CSCE 222/ECEN 222; junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon successful completion of the course, students will be able to:

Explain a variety of fundamental algorithms and algorithmic techniques

Select the most appropriate algorithm to solve a given problem

Analyze the correctness, runtime, and space complexity of an algorithm

Design new algorithms and modify existing ones using techniques discussed

in class

Recognize when a problem is likely intractable (has no efficient algorithm)

and be able to prove this by showing undecidability or NP-hardness

Special Course Designation

None

Introduction to Algorithms, fourth edition

ISBN:  9780262046305

Design new algorithms and modify existing ones using techniques discussed

in class

Recognize when a problem is likely intractable (has no efficient algorithm)

and be able to prove this by showing undecidability or NP-hardness

ISBN:  9780262046305

Authors:  Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson,

Ronald L. Rivest, Clifford Stein

Publisher:  MIT Press

Publication Date:  2022-04-05

Grading Policy

Standard Letter Grading Scale:  A >= 90 > B >= 80 > C >= 70 > D >= 60 > F

Graded components:

Exams - 45%: There will be 3 (2 midterms + 1 final) equally weighted exams

Homework - 40%: There will be 8 equally weighted homework

Recall Practices - 10%: Short quizzes to practice recalling information

Class activities - 5%: We will use different types of activities to help you better

understand the concepts and connect with the materials in class.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Exams: Make-up tests will only be given if the student has an excused

absence (either university-excused or instructor-excused) with supporting

documentation.

Homework: Assignments turned in after the posted deadline will have a

penalty applied of 5% per day late.

Recall Practice and In-Class Activities: These are completed in class and thus

cannot be accepted late. They may be excused if a student has an excused

absence (either university-excused or instructor-excused) with supporting

documentation.

Course Schedule

Below is the planned but tentative schedule of topics and major assignments/exams. Note

that there will be homework assignments, recall practice, and in-class activities throughout

as well. All assignment and exam details will be communicated via Canvas.

Week 1 (January 12): Introduction, Asymptotic Analysis Review (Module 1)

Week 2 (January 19): Lower Bound on Sorting (Module 2)

Course Schedule

Below is the planned but tentative schedule of topics and major assignments/exams. Note

that there will be homework assignments, recall practice, and in-class activities throughout

as well. All assignment and exam details will be communicated via Canvas.

Week 1 (January 12): Introduction, Asymptotic Analysis Review (Module 1)

Week 2 (January 19): Lower Bound on Sorting (Module 2)

Week 3 (January 26):  Divide and Conquer (Module 3)

Week 4 (February 2): Divide and Conquer (Module 3), Dynamic Programming

(Module 4)

Week 5 (February 9): Dynamic Programming (Module 4)

Week 6 (February 16):  Exam 1 , Greedy Algorithms (Module 5)

Week 7 (February 23): Greedy Algorithms (Module 5), Amortized Analysis

(Module 6)

Week 8 (March 2): Disjoint Sets (Module 7), Randomized Algorithms (Module

8)

Week of March 9-13: Spring Break - No classes

Week 9 (March 16): Randomized Algorithms (Module 8), Graph Algorithms

(Module 9)

Week 10 (March 23):  Exam 2 , Graph Algorithms (Module 9)

Week 11 (March 30): Graph Algorithms (Module 9)

Week 12 (April 6): Complexity Theory (Module 10)

Week 13 (April 13): Complexity Theory (Module 10), Approximation Algorithms

(Module 11)

Week 14 (April 20): Undecidability (Module 12), Semester Review

Week 15 (April 26):  Final Exam

Important dates:

Exam 1: Tuesday February 17, 2026, in class

Exam 2: Tuesday March 24, 2026, in class

Final Exam: Thursday April 30, 2026, 12:30 pm - 2:30 pm

AI Statement

This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students

themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any

substantive portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a

company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.
